attempts.student_answer:
Okay, this is an "explain" task, not an "argue" task — the verb is "explain," so I need a controlling idea (focus + previews the parts + no side), not a claim with a side. The prompt basically hands me my three parts: what causes tides, how often they happen, and why the timing shifts each day. One problem: I don't actually have the article "The Rhythm of the Tides" in front of me, so I can't quote its exact sentences. I'll write the essay the way the course taught me and mark where the article's evidence would go, using what the article almost certainly says about the moon and tides.

The Rhythm of the Tides

If you sit on a beach all day, you can watch the water slowly creep up the sand and then slide back out again. This rise and fall is called the tide, and it follows a pattern people have tracked for centuries. Ocean tides can be understood through three connected parts: the pull of the moon's gravity that causes them, the roughly twice-daily cycle they follow, and the small daily shift in their timing caused by the moon's own movement around Earth.

First, tides are caused mainly by the moon's gravity. According to the article, the moon's gravitational pull tugs on Earth's oceans, making the water bulge out on the side of Earth facing the moon, with a matching bulge on the opposite side. The article also notes that the sun's gravity plays a smaller role. This matters for understanding tides because the water isn't rising on its own — it is being pulled by something far away in space, which is why the pattern is so regular and predictable.

Second, tides follow a steady schedule. The article explains that most coastlines experience two high tides and two low tides in about a 24-hour period, roughly six hours apart. This happens because as Earth rotates, each spot on the coast passes through both bulges of water once a day. In other words, the daily spin of the Earth is what turns the moon's pull into a repeating rhythm that sailors and fishermen can plan around.

Third, the timing of the tides shifts a little each day. The article points out that high tide arrives about 50 minutes later each day. This is because the moon is not standing still — it is orbiting Earth, so Earth has to rotate a bit extra each day to "catch up" to where the moon has moved. That extra rotation takes close to an hour, which is why a high tide at noon today will come closer to 12:50 tomorrow.

In the end, the tides are not random at all. The moon's gravity creates them, Earth's rotation makes them repeat about twice a day, and the moon's slow orbit nudges their timing later each day. Once you see how these three parts fit together, the rising and falling of the ocean stops being a mystery and becomes a clock written in water.

(Honest note as a student: since the actual article text wasn't shown to me, my "according to the article" evidence is my best guess at what it says. If I had the real article, I would swap in its exact facts and numbers with attributive tags like the course taught me in the quoting/paraphrasing lessons.)

attempts.student_answer:
Okay, first thing I did was read the verb — it says "explain," not "argue," so this is an informational essay: no side, no opinion, just a controlling idea that sets a focus and previews the parts. The prompt basically hands me the three parts: (1) how the hive works, (2) how bees pollinate crops, (3) why bee health matters to the food supply. One problem: I can't actually see the article "The Work of the Honeybee" in front of me, so I can't pull exact quotes. I'll write the essay the way the course taught me and mark where the article's specific evidence would go with what I remember bees-wise — on a real test I'd swap in the article's exact facts with attributive tags.

Here's my composition:

Honeybees do far more than make honey. According to the article "The Work of the Honeybee," honeybees keep the food supply running through three connected jobs: the organized work inside the hive, the pollination of crops, and the protection of the harvests people depend on.

Inside the hive, every bee has a specific role. The article explains that a single queen lays the eggs, thousands of worker bees gather nectar and build the wax comb, and drones exist to mate with the queen. Workers even change jobs as they age, starting as nurses for the young and later becoming foragers who fly out for food. This division of labor matters because no single bee could survive alone; the hive works like one organism, with each job supporting the next. Because the hive depends on so many workers doing narrow tasks, anything that kills workers weakens the whole system.

That system is what makes pollination possible. When a forager lands on a flower to collect nectar, the article notes that grains of pollen stick to the tiny hairs on its body. As the bee moves from blossom to blossom, it rubs that pollen onto the next flower, fertilizing it so the plant can produce fruit and seeds. In other words, pollination is an accident of the bee's food-gathering — but it is an accident farms are built on. A single hive's foragers can visit thousands of flowers in one day, which means one healthy hive can pollinate whole fields.

This is why honeybee health is tied directly to the food supply. The article states that many crops people eat — including apples, almonds, and squash — depend heavily on bee pollination, and that farmers even rent hives to pollinate their fields. When hives collapse from disease, pesticides, or lost habitat, fewer flowers get pollinated, harvests shrink, and food costs rise. Because the hive, the pollination, and the harvest form one chain, damage to the first link is felt at the dinner table.

From the buzzing order inside the hive, to the pollen carried flower to flower, to the fruit on grocery shelves, honeybees connect their work to ours. The article makes clear that understanding how bees live is really understanding how we eat.

Self-check, like the course drilled: my controlling idea sets a focus (honeybees and the food supply), previews all three parts, and takes no side — passes the explain test. Each body paragraph has evidence with an attributive tag ("the article explains/notes/states") plus reasoning that doesn't just restate the fact (that's my usual warrant weakness, so I checked each one on purpose — the "because the hive depends on workers..." and "damage to the first link" sentences are me connecting, not repeating). Honest note: since the actual article text wasn't shown to me, my "evidence" is generic bee facts in the article's voice, not real quotes — the structure is what the course taught, but the specific evidence would need the real article.

Okay, the verb here is "explain," so this is an informational essay, not an argument — no side, just a focus and parts. I did a full informational essay in lesson 24, so I'll use that structure: intro with a controlling idea that previews the parts, body paragraphs with evidence + reasoning, transitions, and a conclusion. One problem: I can only see the one fact the question gives me ("about one mouthful in three of our diet benefits from honeybee pollination"), not the whole article, so I'll build my evidence around that quote and be honest that it's my main piece of evidence.

Here's my essay:

Honeybees are small insects, but their work touches almost everything people eat. According to the article "The Work of the Honeybee," a decline in honeybees would matter to ordinary people because bees affect the food supply, food prices, and the variety of foods on our plates.

First, honeybees are directly connected to how much food we have. The article reports that "about one mouthful in three of our diet benefits from honeybee pollination." That statistic means honeybees are not just making honey — roughly a third of what an ordinary person eats each day exists partly because a bee pollinated a plant. If honeybee numbers dropped, the crops that depend on their pollination would produce less, which means less food for everyone, not just for farmers or scientists.

In addition, a drop in the food supply would hit people's wallets. When pollination goes down, farmers harvest smaller amounts of fruits, vegetables, and nuts. Smaller harvests make those foods scarcer, and scarcer foods cost more at the grocery store. This matters because ordinary families would pay higher prices for everyday items, so a problem that starts in a beehive ends up showing in a family's food budget.

Finally, fewer honeybees would shrink the variety of foods people enjoy. The one-in-three-mouthfuls figure covers many of the fresh foods that make meals healthy and interesting, like fruits and vegetables. Without enough bees to pollinate them, some of these foods could become rare. That would leave ordinary people with duller, less nutritious choices, since the foods that don't need bee pollination, like some grains, would make up more of the diet.

In conclusion, the article shows that honeybees do quiet work with loud consequences. Because about one in every three mouthfuls of our diet depends on their pollination, a decline in honeybees would mean less food, higher prices, and fewer choices for ordinary people. The health of a bee colony turns out to be the business of everyone who eats.

Honest self-check: I stuck to explaining (no "should" or side-taking, I think), I used an attributive tag ("According to the article..."), my controlling idea previews the three parts, and each body paragraph has evidence or reasoning plus a "why it matters to ordinary people" sentence — that last part is basically the warrant move I always struggle with, so I tried hard not to just restate the fact. My worry is that the question says to use "specific evidence from the article" (plural-ish?) and I really only had the one statistic to quote, so paragraphs two and three lean on my own reasoning more than on article evidence. If the real test showed me the full article, I'd pull different facts for each paragraph like lesson 11 taught with two pieces of evidence.
